data.title: Sen. Warren calls on Fed's Cook to activate capital buffer tool for banks
data.description:
U.S. Senator Elizabeth Warren (D-MA) pressed Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook to deploy the so-called countercyclical capital buffer tool, warning that President Donald Trump's erratic tariff policies might hinder the banking system and broader economy, according to a media report dated Thursday.

Another area of concern, Warren wrote in a letter to Cook, is growing vulnerabilities in private credit markets since the great recession over a decade ago, Bloomberg reported.

The capital buffer in question was authorized by Congress for the Fed to require banks to hold extra capital during economic expansions to bolster their resilience against potential future downturns.

Although banks have consistently claimed that stricter regulations boost lending costs and disadvantage them relative to global rivals, Warren dismissed the notion that easing rules would hurt lending and harm banks.

“Better capitalized banks lend more to businesses and households and are better positioned to buoy the economy in a recession – a reality growing increasingly likely in light of President Trump’s economic mismanagement,” Warren said in the letter, as quoted by Bloomberg. “The Fed is inexplicably moving in the opposite direction, weakening big bank safeguards at the worst possible moment.”

The Fed considers a number of factors in assessing financial stability and whether to activate the countercyclical capital buffer, including asset-liability mismatch, leverage and asset values.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: NN, Inc. reiterates $430M–$460M sales guidance while targeting 13%–14% EBITDA margin amid portfolio shift
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NN, Inc. (NNBR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Harold C. Bevis, CEO, opened the call stating, “We had a pretty solid quarter. Our sales were right at $107.9 million. That's adjusted for the sale of Lubbock last year. Our adjusted EBITDA came in at $13.2 million, which was 12.2% of sales. Our operating income -- adjusted operating income came in at $4.9 million and our adjusted net income was $0.02 per diluted share.”
  • Bevis highlighted nearly 20% adjusted gross margins, “We got really close to 20%, 19.5% adjusted gross margins.” He also emphasized new business momentum: “We gained a lot of new business for future periods, $32.7 million year-to-date, which put us on pace for our annual goal this year of $65 million.”
  • Bevis reported a sales portfolio shift: “39% of our sales was in automotive and 61% non-automotive. So that's a strategic goal of ours is to balance our portfolio.”
  • Strategic refinancing was noted: “We refinanced our term loan and we're now focused fully on reducing the cost of our term loan as well as refinancing our preferred stock.”
  • Market dynamics were addressed, including global automotive turbulence, the resurgence of internal combustion engine (ICE) programs, and growing commercial vehicle and medical equipment segments.
  • COO Timothy M. French described operational transformation, stating, “Since June 2023, we reduced our staffing by more than 600 or approximately 20%. However, this is not exclusively about reduction. Although we reduced by more than 700, we also strategically added approximately 80 people in those areas where we are seeing the most growth and to also increase our talent base in areas of the company such as sales and engineering.”
  • French outlined margin progress: “Year-to-date, our 18.2% adjusted gross margin is an expansion of 190 basis points. We'll continue to focus on further margin expansion. As part of our longer term plan, we have an internal goal to achieve approximately 20% gross margin, which based on our current results, we believe is achievable.”
  • CFO Christopher H. Bohnert stated, “On an as-reported basis, net sales for the quarter were $107.9 million, declining about $15.1 million versus last year's second quarter. On a pro forma basis, accounting for the adjustments I noted earlier, net sales modestly declined 2.4% or $2.7 million.”
  • Bohnert added, “Our adjusted operating income for the second quarter was $4.9 million, marking a strong increase of $2.8 million compared to the $2.1 million in the prior year second quarter.”
  • Management announced the hiring of a new Chief Commercial Officer, Tim Erro, and a new CTO, enhancing focus on electrical and medical segments. Bevis revealed, “we now have a core team who understands electrical cable assemblies and we're evaluating an organic entry into that market just as we've done for medical products.”

Outlook

  • Bohnert reiterated guidance: “Net sales, we're still expecting in the range of $430 million to $460 million, adjusted EBITDA of $53 million to $63 million. However, we are leaning toward the lower half of the range on both those guidances.”
  • Free cash flow guidance remained at $14 million to $16 million. “New business wins, again, no change, $60 million to $70 million.”
  • Management cautioned that guidance “reflects the uncertainty from some of our top customers that we're hearing in the marketplace as well as the unstable macroeconomic environment, but we are holding our guidance at this point.”

Financial Results

  • Net sales for Q2 were $107.9 million. Adjusted EBITDA was $13.2 million. Adjusted operating income stood at $4.9 million, and adjusted net income was $0.02 per diluted share.
  • Adjusted gross margin reached 19.5%. Adjusted EBITDA margin was 12.2% of net sales, expanding by 130 basis points as reported.
  • Power Solutions segment net sales were $44.6 million, with adjusted EBITDA at $9.1 million and margin of 20.4%. Mobile Solutions segment net sales were $63.4 million, adjusted EBITDA $8.6 million, with margin climbing to 13.6%.
  • Year-to-date, 70 new programs launched; management expects 112 new programs in 2025, contributing $26 million to this year’s top line and $48 million at peak run rate. The new business pipeline reached $750 million.
  • Capital expenditures are slated at $18 million to $20 million for the year, with a focus on growth in medical and China auto markets.

Q&A

  • Robert Duncan Brown, Lake Street Capital: Asked about incremental margin on new business wins and ability to take share. Bevis explained, “for new business wins… if we have existing open capacity and our costs are fully covered in the plants. And we price that to win. We'll go down to 15% on that type of a business…for any new investments, we have our floor at 25% ROI for the investments…it's accretive by 3 or 4 points on the EBITDA line overall.”
  • Brown inquired about electrical and data center markets. Bevis shared, “on the power generation side, where we directly participate in that, we have been growing. So overall, it's a growth area for our company.”
  • John Edward Franzreb, Sidoti & Company: Probed for drivers to reach low-end guidance. Bevis clarified, “We're going to be benefiting though from our new business launches…we're counting on those programs not getting pushed out.”
  • Franzreb asked about capital expenditures. French responded, “The bulk of it is in new business programs… the vast majority of it is to support the growth.”
  • Michael Roy Crawford, B. Riley Securities: Requested specifics on on-time delivery improvements. French said, “we now have green scorecards with all of our customers, meaning that we are a supplier in good standing, and in some cases, identified as a preferred supplier.”
  • Crawford sought capacity details for dedicated medical machines. Bevis explained, “The business right now is $15 million to $18 million run rate. So 2 shift is normal for us. So right away, just in terms of normal, we have double the amount of sales possibility there, $30 million to $35 million.”
  • Hans Baldau, NOBLE Capital: Asked about M&A program details. Bevis noted, “We are being very -- I'm leading it myself personally. We're being very specific with the type of acquisitions we're looking at. We have several active processes underway.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on guidance achievability, capital allocation, and growth areas. Questions pointed to uncertainties in market timing and execution risk, especially around new program launches and M&A.
  • Management’s tone remained confident and measured during prepared remarks, with Bevis frequently emphasizing progress and transformation. In Q&A, management displayed transparency, detailed responses, and readiness to address challenges, but also acknowledged risks: “It's possible that if people get nervous, they could push out launch dates on us and we've had a little bit of that this year.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a confident yet realistic tone, reiterating guidance but increasingly noting external uncertainties. Analysts continued to probe execution risk and the pace of transformation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for net sales and adjusted EBITDA was reiterated, but management signaled a tilt toward the lower end, citing customer and macroeconomic uncertainties—a nuanced shift from the prior quarter’s firmer outlook.
  • Strategic focus remains on portfolio diversification, operational efficiency, and new business wins, with incremental progress in staffing, new leadership, and capital deployment for targeted growth areas.
  • Analysts sustained their focus on margin improvement, capital allocation, and the execution pace of new business launches, consistent with previous quarter’s questions.
  • Key metrics such as gross margin and EBITDA margin further improved, while the number of new program launches was slightly reduced due to timing adjustments in the automotive sector.
  • Management confidence in transformation efforts remains high, with an uptick in detail about M&A strategy and organic market entries.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified uncertainties in the global automotive market, including “cloudiness or uncertainty with the tariffs, vehicle affordability, high interest rates, fading electric vehicle incentives and the emergence of China as the global exporter of choice.”
  • The risk of delayed new program launches was cited as a key factor that could impact revenue guidance.
  • Sticky working capital balances, driven by rising metal prices, continue to challenge cash flow, though management outlined plans to reduce working capital as a percentage of sales.
  • Analysts raised concerns about execution risk around capital expenditures, new market entries, and the pace of M&A integration.

Final Takeaway

NN, Inc. management emphasized steady progress in its transformation strategy, with further margin improvements, a balanced sales portfolio, and ongoing cost discipline. The company remains confident in achieving its full-year targets while acknowledging external headwinds and execution risks, particularly in automotive and new business launches. Expansion into medical and electrical markets, alongside a revitalized M&A strategy and leadership additions, positions NN for continued growth, supported by robust new business wins and a growing pipeline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nasdaq turns red, stocks extend losses after weak bond auction
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages on Thursday turned lower, as positive sentiment sparked by U.S. President Donald Trump's chip tariff exemptions and progress on Russia-Ukraine de-escalation petered out.

A $25B 30-year Treasury bond auction tailed, signaling weak demand for fixed-income securities and further weighing on investors' moods. It was the third straight weak Treasury auction this week.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 2 basis points to 4.25%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was up 2 basis points to 3.74%.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.4% in afternoon trade, giving up an advance of as much as +0.7%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.7%. 

The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) also slipped into negative territory, last -0.2%, after steadily paring gains. It had earlier been boosted by a post-earnings climb in AppLovin (APP) and DoorDash (DASH), and extended gains in tech titan Apple (AAPL). The iPhone-maker on Wednesday confirmed a new $100B investment in the U.S.

The tech-heavy index was also boosted by several chip stocks, as investors took heart from Trump's vow to exempt semiconductor manufacturers from a planned 100% tariff on imports if they made their products in the United States.

President Trump “won’t charge tariffs while you’re building in the country, but if you’re not building in the country, 100% tariffs,” Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick told Fox Business in the morning.

Shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), the world's largest contract chipmaker, clinched a record high on Thursday after Taiwan said TSM will not have to pay the 100% tariff.

Speaking of tariffs, reciprocal barriers that had been postponed twice finally went into effect against U.S. trading partners across the world on Wednesday, bringing the average tariff rate to levels not seen since World War II.   

“Is this the new global tariff regime? Not quite yet. While some countries are still negotiating with the U.S., America’s new tariff regime entered into effect at midnight, increasing the effective U.S. tariff level from just under 3% at the beginning of the year to just over 15%,” said Mohamed A. El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz and former CEO of PIMCO.

“Up to this point, many companies appear to have used inventories and profit margins as buffers, as well as pressure on suppliers, while waiting to see what would finally emerge. Now, they must make more challenging, longer-term decisions about whether and how much to adjust prices,” El-Erian added. 

Also grabbing headlines were signs of progress on a truce between Russia and Ukraine.

"We had some very good talks with President Putin today, and there's a very good chance that we could be ending the round — the end of that road. There's a good chance that there will be a meeting very soon," Trump told reporters on Wednesday.

Meanwhile, The New York Times reported that Trump was planning to hold in-person meetings with Russian and Ukrainian leaders Vladimir Putin and Volodymyr Zelenskyy as soon as next week. 

Turning to Thursday's economic calendar, initial jobless claims jumped more than expected, while continuing claims rose to their highest mark since 2021.

data.title: Aareal Bank AG reports 1H results

  • Outlook

data.title: Crocs outlines Q3 revenue decline of 9%-11% amid cautious consumer and strategic resets
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Crocs, Inc. (CROX) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Andrew Rees opened by emphasizing "Our teams delivered a solid second quarter, fueled by top line growth and our highest ever quarterly gross profit, which drove strong free cash flow in the midst of what continues to be a volatile marketplace." He reported second quarter revenues of $1.1 billion, with Crocs Brand revenues at $960 million and HEYDUDE at $190 million. International growth for Crocs Brand reached 16%. Rees highlighted "pull back on promotional activity across the direct channels starting in May" for Crocs and accelerated cleanup actions for HEYDUDE, stating these would "impact our top line," but are intended to "drive margin dollars over time, support continued cash flow generation and tighten brand control." He also noted $50 million of cost savings already actioned, further cost-cutting initiatives under review, and a focus on conservative inventory planning.
  • Rees underscored diversification, with international now over half of Crocs Brand revenue and the addition of new product pillars beyond clogs, notably in sandals and personalization.
  • CFO Susan Healy stated, "Our second quarter enterprise revenues of $1.1 billion were up 3% to prior year... Enterprise adjusted gross margins of 61.7% were up 30 basis points to prior year." She further noted, "This excludes the noncash impairment charge of $737 million on HEYDUDE's intangible assets. This impairment comes as a result of a longer-than-expected time line to stabilize the HEYDUDE Brand and return it to growth."

Outlook

  • Healy provided, "For Q3 we expect consolidated revenues to be in the range of down 9% to 11% at currency rates as of August 4. This revenue range is based on the visibility we have to orders from our wholesale partners, a reduction of discounts in our Crocs DTC channels, the pullback of performance marketing for HEYDUDE, the incremental cleanup actions we have elected to take for HEYDUDE as well as the potential range of outcomes against a weakening U.S. consumer backdrop."
  • Within this range, Crocs Brand is expected to be "down mid-single digits, led by declines in North America, offset in part by growth in international."
  • Adjusted operating margin is projected to be "in a range of 18% to 19%, including an anticipated approximate 170-basis point impact from tariffs and deleverage on expenses tied to our reduced revenue outlook."
  • Management is "not reinstating full year guidance at this time."

Financial Results

  • Crocs Brand revenue grew to $960 million, up 4% year over year, with international revenue up 16%. North America revenue was down 6%, attributed to a strategic pullback on discounting. HEYDUDE revenue was $190 million, down 4% year over year, with DTC up 7% and wholesale down 13%.
  • Enterprise adjusted gross margin reached 61.7%. Crocs Brand adjusted gross margin was 64.1%, flat year over year. HEYDUDE Brand adjusted gross margin improved to 50.2%.
  • Adjusted SG&A rate rose to 34.7%. Adjusted operating margin was 26.9%. Adjusted diluted EPS was $4.23.
  • Inventory balance as of June 30 was $405 million, up 7% to prior year. Liquidity included $201 million in cash and $784 million in borrowing capacity. Share repurchases totaled 1.3 million shares for $133 million.
  • A $737 million non-cash impairment charge on HEYDUDE's intangible assets was recorded.

Q&A

  • Jonathan Komp, Baird: Asked about Crocs North America outlook and management changes. Rees explained the impact of cautious consumers, pullback on discounting, and highlighted "significant product innovation on sandal in the first half of next year." He acknowledged strengthening the management team with new appointments.
  • Komp followed up on market share and profitability. Rees discussed "athletic trend in the marketplace" as a headwind and noted, "We've taken out $50 million of cost savings already."
  • Chris Nardone, BofA: Queried on international business sustainability. Rees confirmed, "A mid-teens growth trajectory for our international business, we feel very good about that." On Crocs DTC outlook, Rees stated, "We're not baking in it getting sequentially worse as it goes through the quarter."
  • Nardone asked about pricing and tariffs. Rees said, "We have selective actions planned, some in the back end of this year for HEYDUDE... we will be announcing some price increases on Crocs, both in select styles and also in some of our international markets."
  • Adrienne Yih, Barclays: Asked about the Q3 revenue decline drivers. Rees replied, "The guide embeds the order book for both brands... it also embeds conservative assumptions around returns or cancellations."
  • Anna Andreeva, Piper Sandler: Asked about promotional activity and shelf space. Rees noted a "meaningful impact" from reduced discounting and discussed "accelerated" shelf space losses to athletic brands.
  • Additional analyst questions covered cost savings, inventory, product innovation, HEYDUDE leadership, and capital allocation.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on the sustainability of international growth, the impact of tariffs, share losses to athletic brands, and the risks to margins, reflecting a slightly negative to neutral tone. Questions often returned to profitability protection, demand visibility, and the effectiveness of strategic resets.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident and proactive, emphasizing strategic cost controls, innovation, and long-term brand health. In Q&A, management acknowledged challenges but maintained composure: "We think we're doing the right things for the long-term potential of the brand." Defensive and cautious language appeared regarding guidance and consumer uncertainty.
  • Compared to Q1, analysts expressed heightened skepticism around revenue headwinds and the effectiveness of mitigation strategies, while management was more defensive and detailed about cost controls and downside scenarios.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language has shifted from "withdrawing guidance" in Q1 to offering a specific Q3 outlook, but still no full-year guidance reinstated.
  • Strategic focus has moved further toward cost savings, inventory control, and margin protection, with deeper pullbacks on promotions and marketing spend, especially for HEYDUDE.
  • Analyst questions in Q2 centered more on revenue outlook, brand health, and competitive pressures, especially from athletic brands.
  • Key metrics show Crocs Brand revenue growth slowing in North America, while international growth accelerated. HEYDUDE's sales decline has moderated, but profitability concerns have intensified following the impairment charge.
  • Management's confidence in brand diversification and international expansion remains, but there is greater caution and defensiveness in tone compared to Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued weakness in U.S. consumer discretionary spending and a "volatile marketplace."
  • Tariffs remain a significant risk, with an estimated $40 million impact in the second half of 2025 and $90 million annually under current rates.
  • Inventory levels have risen, partly due to tariffs, and management is monitoring for returns and cancellations.
  • Analysts voiced concerns over shelf space loss to athletic competitors, the sustainability of cost savings, and the timeline for a HEYDUDE turnaround.

Final Takeaway

Crocs management emphasized that despite top line pressures and an uncertain consumer environment, decisive actions on cost savings, inventory planning, and brand health are intended to support long-term profitability. The company is prioritizing strategic resets, especially in North America and with HEYDUDE, while leveraging international momentum and product innovation to drive future growth. The Q3 outlook signals a challenging period ahead, with management focused on margin protection and operational discipline amid ongoing volatility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fidelity National Financial outlines 15%–20% pretax title margin target while expanding commercial volume and capital allocation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Fidelity National Financial (FNF) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Michael Joseph Nolan stated, "Overall, our businesses generated strong results for the second quarter." He highlighted adjusted pretax title earnings of $337 million, a 4% increase over the second quarter of 2024, and pointed to an industry-leading adjusted pretax title margin of 15.5%, up 380 basis points from the first quarter of 2025. Nolan also called out a 5% increase in daily purchase orders opened over the first quarter of 2025 and a 28% increase in refinance orders compared to the prior year. Commercial volumes were described as a bright spot, with direct commercial revenue of $626 million in the first six months, up 23% over the first half of 2024. Nolan noted, "Our Title segment remains poised for a rebound in transaction volumes, and we continue to invest in the business for the long term."

  • CFO Anthony John Park reported, "We generated $3.6 billion in total revenue in the second quarter. Excluding net recognized gains and losses, our total revenue was $3.5 billion as compared with $3.2 billion in the second quarter of 2024." Park cited net earnings of $278 million, including net recognized gains of $98 million, and adjusted net earnings of $318 million or $1.16 per diluted share. The Title segment contributed $260 million in adjusted net earnings, while the F&G segment contributed $89 million. Park detailed that the Title segment generated $2.2 billion in total revenue, excluding net recognized gains, with direct premiums up 12% and agency premiums up 7%. Personnel costs and other operating expenses each increased 10%. Park also highlighted the launch of a new reinsurance vehicle in partnership with Blackstone-managed funds, providing approximately $1 billion in capital commitments for F&G.

Outlook

  • Nolan stated, "Our Title segment remains poised for a rebound in transaction volumes, and we continue to invest in the business for the long term. Over time, we see opportunities to gain efficiencies across our operations and further enhance profitability." Park affirmed, "We don't expect these incremental expenses to impact our ability to deliver a 15% to 20% pretax title margin once we rebound to a normalized market, although transactional volumes remain low at this time."

  • The company expects quarterly interest and investment income of $90 million to $95 million in each quarter for the remainder of 2025, assuming two Fed funds rate cuts. Approximately $28 million per quarter of common and preferred dividend income from F&G to the Corporate segment is also anticipated.

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for the second quarter was $3.6 billion, with adjusted net earnings of $318 million or $1.16 per diluted share. The Title segment generated $2.2 billion in total revenue, with direct premiums increasing by 12% and agency premiums by 7% over the prior year. Adjusted pretax title earnings were $337 million, with an adjusted pretax title margin of 15.5%.

  • Commercial revenue in the first six months reached $626 million, a 23% increase over the prior year, and F&G reported assets under management of $69.2 billion as of June 30, up 13% year-over-year. F&G's gross sales were $4.1 billion, with net sales retained of $2.7 billion. FNF repurchased 2.9 million shares for $159 million in the quarter and returned nearly $300 million of capital to shareholders.

Q&A

  • Mark Christian DeVries, Deutsche Bank: Questioned the appeal of holding F&G within FNF. Park responded that the Board is pleased with F&G's performance and the strategy remains to operate the businesses as they have, noting excitement about the shift to a more fee-based, higher-margin, less capital-intensive business model for F&G.

  • DeVries also asked about the step-up in personnel expenses. Nolan stated it was due to "one of our best recruiting quarters in a long time" and significant strategic hiring, explaining that recruiting expenses are front-loaded but expected to contribute to future revenue. Park cited a $12 million increase in medical claims impacting personnel costs and outlined plans to address benefit design in the following year.

  • Terry Ma, Barclays: Asked about the sustainability of title margins amid elevated expenses. Nolan replied, "We we're right in line really with where we were last year... our base case has always been as we've gone into the year, that '25 is going to be a lot like '24... we think it's going to look a lot like the second half of '24 with the wildcards really being more what happens with mortgage rates up or down."

  • Bose Thomas George, KBW: Inquired about share buybacks and capital allocation. Park replied, "Any weakness in our share price at these levels is a great use of excess capital," adding that incremental capital for F&G is not expected to be needed.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on expense management, capital allocation, and the sustainability of margins, expressing a slightly cautious tone especially around rising personnel costs and margin sustainability.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, emphasizing long-term investments, margin targets, and capital strength. Direct quotes such as "Absolutely, Terry" and "We remain pleased with F&G's performance" reinforced management's confidence.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remains confident but with increased focus on managing elevated expenses. Analyst tone remains cautiously optimistic but more probing on expense trends and capital deployment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter showed an improved adjusted pretax title margin, rising from 11.7% in Q1 to 15.5% in Q2. Direct premiums and agency premiums in the Title segment continued to increase, and commercial revenue growth accelerated.

  • Strategic focus shifted toward long-term margin efficiency, robust recruiting, and capital-light growth for F&G. Share repurchase activity increased, as did capital returned to shareholders. Management's confidence in delivering 15%–20% title margins remains, with additional commentary on expense normalization and strategic investments.

  • Analysts concentrated questions on recruiting, expenses, and buybacks, compared to a prior focus on M&A and investment income cadence.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited elevated health claims as a primary driver of increased expenses, noting these are expected to remain high through the year but normalize in 2026. Strategic investments in technology and security also contributed to higher costs.

  • Analysts pressed on the potential impact of rising expenses on margins and the sustainability of buybacks. Management outlined mitigation strategies, including reviewing benefit plan designs and ongoing efficiency initiatives.

Final Takeaway

Fidelity National Financial delivered strong second quarter results with notable growth in commercial title revenue and sustained industry-leading margins, despite elevated expenses from health claims and strategic investments. Management remains committed to its 15%–20% pretax title margin target and signals continued focus on efficiency, capital allocation, and long-term growth opportunities across both the Title and F&G segments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tech Voices: Trump vs. Intel CEO, Amazon's $1B fed discount
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of remarks, statements and announcements that could impact the technology sector.

  • President Trump called for Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) CEO Lip-Bu Tan to immediately resign over his alleged ties to China.

"The CEO of INTEL is highly CONFLICTED and must resign, immediately. There is no other solution to this problem. Thank you for your attention to this problem!" Trump wrote Thursday on Truth Social.

  • Trump's post comes after U.S. Sen. Tom Cotton questioned Tan's ties to Chinese companies and a past legal case involving a company Tan previously led, Cadence Design (CDNS). 

Cotton reportedly wrote Intel's (NASDAQ:INTC) chairman this week expressing "concern about the security and integrity of Intel’s operations and its potential impact on U.S. national security," according to CNBC.

“Intel is required to be a responsible steward of American taxpayer dollars and to comply with applicable security regulations,” Cotton added. “Mr. Tan’s associations raise questions about Intel’s ability to fulfill these obligations.”

  • Amazon Web Services (NASDAQ:AMZN) will give discounts of up to $1 billion for cloud adoption, modernization, and training services provided to U.S. federal agencies through the end of 2028.

“The Trump Administration is rapidly modernizing out-of-date government systems, streamlining processes, and delivering savings to the American taxpayer through centralized procurement,” said Michael Rigas, acting administrator of the General Services Administration, in a statement. 

"Through this new agreement with AWS, federal agencies will be able to enhance delivery of critical services, leverage cloud and advanced AI technologies, and dramatically reduce costs," Rigas added.

The AWS agreement will provide up to a total of $1 billion in direct incentive credits aggregated across federal civilian agencies, the GSA added.

data.title: Swire Pacific reports 1H results
data.description: • Swire Pacific press release (OTCPK:SWRAY): 1H underlying profit attributable to shareholders of HK$5,476 million.
